## The Potential Outcomes Framework

The **potential outcomes framework** provides the basic language of modern causal inference. Its central idea is simple: to define a causal effect, we must compare what happens to the same unit under alternative treatment states.

The origins of this framework go back to the work of **Jerzy Neyman** on randomized agricultural experiments first published in 1927 [@neyman1990]. Neyman introduced the idea that each experimental unit has potential outcomes under different treatments, even though only one of them is observed. Later, **Donald Rubin** extended this logic beyond randomized experiments, turning it into a general framework for causal inference in both experimental and observational studies [@rubin1974; @rubin1978]. For this reason, the approach is often called the **Neyman--Rubin potential outcomes framework** or the **Rubin causal model**.

Suppose we are interested in the effect of a binary treatment $D_i$ on an outcome $Y_i$ for unit $i$. In the simplest case,

$$
D_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if unit } i \text{ receives the treatment},\\
0 & \text{if unit } i \text{ does not receive the treatment}.
\end{cases}
$$

For each unit, we define two potential outcomes:

$$
Y_i(1)
\quad \text{and} \quad
Y_i(0).
$$

Here, $Y_i(1)$ is the outcome that unit $i$ would experience if treated, while $Y_i(0)$ is the outcome that the same unit would experience if untreated. The individual treatment effect is therefore

$$
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0).
$$

This definition captures the essence of causal inference: a causal effect is the difference between two potential states of the same unit. For example, if the treatment is receiving a public subsidy aimed at reducing workplace accidents, the causal effect for a given firm is the difference between the number of workplace accidents with the subsidy and the number of workplace accidents that the same firm would have experienced without the subsidy.

The problem is that we never observe both potential outcomes for the same unit. If the unit is treated, we observe $Y_i(1)$ but not $Y_i(0)$. If the unit is untreated, we observe $Y_i(0)$ but not $Y_i(1)$. The observed outcome can be written as

$$
Y_i^{obs} = D_iY_i(1) + (1-D_i)Y_i(0).
$$

This means that $Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(1)$ for treated units, for which $D_i=1$, and $Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(0)$ for untreated units, for which $D_i=0$. In other words, we observe only the potential outcome corresponding to the treatment status actually received.

The missing potential outcome is the **counterfactual**. This is why causal inference is fundamentally a missing-data problem: one potential outcome is observed, while the other must be inferred. Holland famously called this the **fundamental problem of causal inference** [@holland1986].

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
### The fundamental problem of causal inference

For each unit, we observe only one potential outcome.  
The other potential outcome is **missing**: it is counterfactual and cannot be directly observed. This missingness is not a data collection accident, but a structural feature of causal inference, because each unit can be observed under either the treatment or the untreated status.
:::

Because individual treatment effects are generally impossible to observe, causal inference usually focuses on average treatment effects ([discussed in Chapter 5](../chapters/04-causal-parameters.qmd)).

The reason is that, although we cannot observe both potential outcomes for the same unit, we can observe different units under different treatment statuses. If treated and untreated units are comparable, the average outcome among untreated units can be used to approximate the missing counterfactual outcome for treated units, and vice versa. In this sense, causal inference replaces the impossible comparison of the same unit in two states of the world with a comparison between groups of units that are credible substitutes for one another.

## Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs)

In [randomized experiments](../chapters/05-randomized-experiments.qmd), treatment assignment is controlled by the researcher. In a successful RCT, treatment status is independent of the units' potential outcomes. Formally, random assignment implies

$$
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
$$

This means that treated and control groups are comparable **on average, not necessarily unit by unit**. The logic of randomization is therefore statistical. By the **law of large numbers**, as the number of units increases, random assignment tends to balance both observed and unobserved characteristics across treatment and control groups.

This also clarifies why randomizing only two units, one treated and one untreated, is not enough to make the RCT logic convincing. Even if treatment is randomly assigned, the two units may still differ substantially for reasons unrelated to the treatment. With only two units, there is no averaging that washes out individual idiosyncrasies or pre-existing differences.

With larger samples, instead, the average outcome of the control group provides an estimate of what the treated group would have experienced without treatment. Similarly, the average outcome of the treated group provides information about outcomes under treatment. In formal terms,

$$
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].
$$

Randomization does not reveal the missing counterfactual for each individual unit. It solves the counterfactual problem **on average**, by creating a credible comparison group that reveals the missing average counterfactual.

This is why average treatment effects can be estimated even though individual treatment effects cannot be directly observed. RCTs are therefore often considered the benchmark for causal inference: by design, they solve the counterfactual problem for average treatment effects [@neyman1990; @rubin1978].

## Observational Studies

In many social science and policy settings, random assignment is not feasible. Individuals, firms, schools, or neighborhoods may select into treatment, or policymakers may target treatment to particular units.

In observational studies, treatment assignment is not controlled by the researcher. Treated and untreated units likely differ in observable and unobservable characteristics before the treatment occurs. These differences may affect the outcome and create **selection bias**. As a result, a simple post-treatment comparison of outcomes between treated and untreated units may capture not only the effect of the treatment, but also pre-existing differences between the two groups.

Rubin's contribution was to show that the same potential outcomes logic can still be used in observational studies [@rubin1974]. The goal is to reconstruct, as closely as possible, the comparison that a randomized experiment would have produced. Different causal methods do this in different ways: matching, weighting, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables, and synthetic control all try to estimate credible counterfactual outcomes under different assumptions.

## Key Assumptions

The potential outcomes framework is not, by itself, an estimator. It defines causal effects and clarifies what must be observed or assumed in order to estimate them. In its basic version, several assumptions are especially important.

### Manipulable treatment

The treatment must correspond to a clear intervention that could, at least in principle, be assigned or manipulated. This idea is often summarized by the motto: **no causation without manipulation**.

For example, attending a training program, receiving a subsidy, or gaining access to a nursery school can be interpreted as treatments. By contrast, characteristics such as nationality at birth or age are not treatments in the same direct sense, because they cannot be assigned by an intervention.

### Consistency

The observed outcome must correspond to the potential outcome associated with the treatment actually received. That is,

$$
Y_i^{obs} = Y_i(1) \quad \text{if } D_i=1,
$$

and

$$
Y_i^{obs} = Y_i(0) \quad \text{if } D_i=0.
$$

Consistency connects the observed data to the potential outcomes. Without it, the notation $Y_i(1)$ and $Y_i(0)$ would not map clearly into the outcomes observed in the data.

### Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption

A central assumption of the basic framework is the **Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption**, usually called **SUTVA** [@rubin1980]. This assumption allows us to write only two potential outcomes for each unit: $Y_i(1)$ and $Y_i(0)$.

SUTVA has two main components.

First, there should be **no hidden versions of the treatment**. The treatment must be sufficiently homogeneous across treated units. If some units receive a light version of the programme and others receive a much more intensive version, then the notation $Y_i(1)$ is too vague. There is not one treatment, but several different treatments.

Second, there should be **no interference between units**. The potential outcome of one unit should depend only on that unit’s own treatment status, not on the treatment status of other units. This idea is often traced back to Cox’s discussion of interference between experimental units [@cox1958].

Formally, in the simple version of the framework, we write

$$
Y_i = Y_i(D_i).
$$

This assumes that unit $i$'s outcome depends only on $D_i$. If instead unit $i$'s outcome also depends on whether other units are treated, the notation would need to be expanded.

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
### When SUTVA may fail

SUTVA may fail when the treatment has different versions or intensities, when treated units affect untreated units, or when there are spillovers, peer effects, displacement effects, or general equilibrium effects.
:::

SUTVA can fail in two main ways:
i) **Different treatment among treated units**. Suppose that a school introduces a tutoring program for students with low test scores. Some students receive one-to-one tutoring, while others receive group tutoring. If we treat all participants as if they had received the same intervention, we are mixing different versions of the treatment. In this case, the estimated effect would not refer to a clearly defined program.
ii) **The treatment received by one unit may affect the outcomes of other units**. Suppose that students who receive tutoring share materials, explain exercises, or study together with classmates who were not selected for the program. In this case, untreated students are no longer unaffected by the intervention: their outcomes may improve because of their interaction with treated students. This violates the no-interference component of SUTVA. The comparison between tutored and non-tutored students would therefore be contaminated: the control group is not simply untreated, but indirectly exposed to the treatment.

### Assignment mechanism

To estimate causal effects, we also need assumptions about how treatment is assigned. In randomized experiments, assignment is random:

$$
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
$$

This means that treatment status is independent of the potential outcomes. In other words, units with high or low values of $Y_i(1)$ or $Y_i(0)$ are not systematically more likely to be assigned to treatment. As a result, the treated and control groups are comparable on average, and the control group can be used to estimate the missing counterfactual outcome for the treated group.

In observational studies, this is usually not true. For instance, the key identifying assumption of matching methods is the **conditional independence assumpion**:

$$
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i \mid X_i.
$$

This means that, after conditioning on observed covariates $X_i$, treatment assignment is as good as random. Under this assumption, treated and untreated units with the same values of $X_i$ can be compared to estimate causal effects.

It is important to recognize that this assumption should generally be considered quite strong. It requires that all variables affecting both treatment assignment and potential outcomes are observed and correctly accounted for.

In other words, after conditioning on $X_i$, there should be no remaining systematic differences between treated and untreated units that are related to the outcome. This is demanding because, in observational studies, treatment assignment often depends on factors that are difficult to measure. For example, workers may enter a training program because they are more motivated, firms may apply for subsidies because they have better managers, and neighborhoods may receive a policy because policymakers expect them to respond more strongly.

If these factors are unobserved and also affect the outcome, then treated and untreated units are still not comparable after conditioning on $X_i$. In that case, the Conditional Independence Assumption fails, and the estimated treatment effect may still reflect selection bias rather than the causal effect of the treatment.

### Overlap

Finally, for comparisons to be meaningful, treated and untreated units must be comparable. This is the **overlap** or **common support** condition.

The idea is simple: for the kinds of units we want to compare, we must observe both treated and untreated cases. If a certain type of unit is always treated, then the data contain no untreated unit of that same type that can be used to approximate its counterfactual outcome. Similarly, if a certain type of unit is never treated, then we cannot learn what treatment would have done for that type of unit from the observed data.

For example, suppose a subsidy is given only to very large firms, while the control group contains only small and medium-sized firms. In that case, the treated firms have no comparable untreated firms. Any comparison between the two groups would mix the effect of the subsidy with pre-existing differences in firm size, resources, and growth prospects.

Overlap therefore asks whether treated and untreated units occupy the same region of the covariate space. When this condition fails, causal inference becomes an extrapolation exercise rather than a comparison between similar units. A common response is to restrict the analysis to the region of common support, where treated and untreated units are actually comparable. However, this generally changes the causal parameter that can be retrieved.

## Summary

The potential outcomes framework defines causal effects by comparing potential outcomes under treatment and no treatment. Its main contribution is to make the counterfactual problem explicit.

For each unit, the individual treatment effect is

$$
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0),
$$

but only one of these two outcomes is observed. This makes individual causal effects generally impossible to measure directly.

Randomized experiments solve this problem by making treatment assignment independent of potential outcomes. Observational studies try to approximate this logic using assumptions and research designs that make treated and untreated units comparable.

The basic framework relies especially on SUTVA: the treatment must be well defined, and one unit's outcome must not depend on the treatment status of other units. When these conditions fail, the simple notation $Y_i(1)$ and $Y_i(0)$ may no longer be sufficient, and the causal question must be reformulated more carefully.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
### Key idea

Causal inference is not just about comparing outcomes.  
It is about comparing observed outcomes with credible counterfactual outcomes.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
### The ladder of causality

Pearl and Mackenzie [-@pearl2018] describe causal reasoning as a **ladder of causality** with three levels.

![Note: The ladder of causality distinguishes between observing associations, evaluating interventions, and reasoning about counterfactuals. The potential outcomes framework operates at the counterfactual level: it asks what would have happened to the same unit under a different treatment status.](../images/ladder-of-causality-wooden.png)

The first level is **association**. At this level, we ask whether two variables move together. For example: are firms that receive public subsidies less likely to have workplace accidents? This is an associational question. It can be answered by comparing observed accident rates, but it does not tell us whether the subsidy caused the difference.

The second level is **intervention**. At this level, we ask what would happen if we actively changed something. For example: what would happen to workplace accidents if a firm received a public subsidy for safety investments? This is the level of causal effects and policy evaluation.

The third level is **counterfactuals**. At this level, we ask what would have happened under an alternative state of the world. For example: for a firm that received the subsidy, how many workplace accidents would have occurred if that same firm had not received it? This is the level of potential outcomes, because it compares the observed outcome with the missing counterfactual outcome.

In this sense, the potential outcomes framework works at the counterfactual level of the ladder. It defines causal effects by comparing what happened with what would have happened under a different treatment status.

:::
